openapi: 3.0.3
info:
  version: 1.1.1
  title: ce-order API
  description: ce-order API
  license:
    name: Richemont
tags:
  - name: Return
    description: Return API
  - name: Order
    description: Operations related to Orders
paths:
  /actuator/health:
    head:
      operationId: healthCheck
      description: Health indicator.
      tags:
        - Monitoring
      responses:
        '200':
          description: API is up.
        '503':
          description: API is unavailable.
  /{tenant}/order:
    $ref: paths/{tenant}@order.yaml
  /{tenant}/return:
    $ref: paths/{tenant}@return.yaml
  /{tenant}/return/reset:
    $ref: paths/{tenant}@return@reset.yaml
  /{tenant}/global-e/checkoutCartInfo:
    $ref: paths/{tenant}@globale@checkoutCartInfo.yaml
components:
  securitySchemes:
    oAuth2:
      $ref: components/securitySchemes/OAuth2.yaml
x-rlg-facets:
  access: private
  exposure: internet
  authentication: client
  cloud_regions:
    - defra-aws
  brand:
    - car
    - vca
  service_level: critical
  service_schedule: always_on
  target_audience: partner
  ownership: group
  information_type:
    - other
  information_class: confidential
  lifecycle: ga
x-rlg-metadata:
  app_name: ric-lrp-ce

post:
  tags:
    - Order
  summary: Create a new order
  description: Copy a CommerceAPI order to CommerceTools.
  security:
    - oAuth2:
        - order:*:*:order:create
  operationId: createOrder
  parameters:
    - name: tenant
      in: path
      required: true
      description: The tenant id (The Maison unique identifier).
      schema:
        type: string
      example: car
    - name: swseOrderId
      in: query
      required: true
      description: The ID of the order from SWSE.
      schema:
        type: integer
  responses:
    '201':
      description: Order created successfully.
      content:
        application/json:
          schema:
            type: object
            description: Confirmation of order creation
    '400':
      description: Bad Request - Invalid input provided.
    '500':
      description: Internal Server Error - Something went wrong on the server.

post:
  tags:
    - Return
  summary: Create a new return for an order
  description: Allows an external system (Extend) to initiate a return for specified items of an order.
  security:
    - oAuth2:
        - order:*:*:order-return:create
  operationId: createReturn
  parameters:
    - name: tenant
      in: path
      required: true
      description: The tenant id (The Maison unique identifier).
      schema:
        type: string
      example: car
  requestBody:
    description: Details of the order and items to be returned.
    required: true
    content:
      application/json:
        schema:
          $ref: '../components/schemas/return/CreateReturnItemsRequest.yaml'


  responses:
    '201':
      description: Return successfully initiated.
    '400':
      description: Bad Request - Invalid input provided (e.g., missing fields, invalid data format, duplicate Ids in request, Id not found in the order).
      content:
        application/json:
          schema:
            $ref: ../components/schemas/ProblemDetail.yaml
    '500':
      description: Internal Server Error - An unexpected error occurred on the server.
      content:
        application/json:
          schema:
            $ref: ../components/schemas/ProblemDetail.yaml

get:
  x-internal: true
  tags:
    - Global-e
  summary: Global-e webhook to retrieve cart content
  description: Returns the content of a cart in the format expected by Global-e
  security:
    - oAuth2:
        - order:{tenant}:*:cart:read
  operationId: getCheckoutCartInfo
  parameters:
    - name: tenant
      in: path
      required: true
      description: The tenant id (The Maison unique identifier).
      schema:
        type: string
      example: car
    - name: cartId
      in: query
      required: true
      description: The cart id
      schema:
        type: string
    - name: validateStock
      in: query
      schema:
        type: boolean

openapi: 3.0.3
info:
  version: 1.1.1
  title: ce-order API
  description: ce-order API
  license:
    name: Richemont
tags:
  - name: Return
    description: Return API
  - name: Order
    description: Operations related to Orders
paths:
  /actuator/health:
    head:
      operationId: healthCheck
      description: Health indicator.
      tags:
        - Monitoring
      responses:
        '200':
          description: API is up.
        '503':
          description: API is unavailable.
  /{tenant}/order:
    post:
      tags:
        - Order
      summary: Create a new order
      description: Copy a CommerceAPI order to CommerceTools.
      security:
        - oAuth2:
            - order:*:*:order:create
      operationId: createOrder
      parameters:
        - name: tenant
          in: path
          required: true
          description: The tenant id (The Maison unique identifier).
          schema:
            type: string
          example: car
        - name: swseOrderId
          in: query
          required: true
          description: The ID of the order from SWSE.
          schema:
            type: integer
      responses:
        '201':
          description: Order created successfully.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                description: Confirmation of order creation
        '400':
          description: Bad Request - Invalid input provided.
        '500':
          description: Internal Server Error - Something went wrong on the server.
  /{tenant}/return:
    post:
      tags:
        - Return
      summary: Create a new return for an order
      description: Allows an external system (Extend) to initiate a return for specified items of an order.
      security:
        - oAuth2:
            - order:*:*:order-return:create
      operationId: createReturn
      parameters:
        - name: tenant
          in: path
          required: true
          description: The tenant id (The Maison unique identifier).
          schema:
            type: string
          example: car
      requestBody:
        description: Details of the order and items to be returned.
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: '#/components/schemas/CreateReturnItemsRequest'
      responses:
        '201':
          description: Return successfully initiated.
        '400':
          description: Bad Request - Invalid input provided (e.g., missing fields, invalid data format, duplicate Ids in request, Id not found in the order).
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/ProblemDetail'
        '500':
          description: Internal Server Error - An unexpected error occurred on the server.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/ProblemDetail'
  /{tenant}/return/reset:
    post:
      summary: Reset specified items in a return by Order ID
      description: Marks specified line items within a return (identified by the Order ID in the request body) as reset.
      operationId: resetReturnItemsByOrder
      tags:
        - Return
      security:
        - oAuth2:
            - order:*:*:order-return:update
      parameters:
        - name: tenant
          in: path
          required: true
          description: The tenant id (The Maison unique identifier).
          schema:
            type: string
          example: car
      requestBody:
        description: Details of the order and line items to reset.
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: '#/components/schemas/ResetReturnItemsRequest'
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Return items reset successfully initiated.
        '400':
          description: Bad Request - Invalid input provided (e.g., missing fields, invalid data format, duplicate Ids in request, Id not found in the order).
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/ProblemDetail'
        '500':
          description: Internal Server Error - An unexpected error occurred on the server.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/ProblemDetail'
  /{tenant}/global-e/checkoutCartInfo:
    get:
      x-internal: true
      tags:
        - Global-e
      summary: Global-e webhook to retrieve cart content
      description: Returns the content of a cart in the format expected by Global-e
      security:
        - oAuth2:
            - order:{tenant}:*:cart:read
      operationId: getCheckoutCartInfo
      parameters:
        - name: tenant
          in: path
          required: true
          description: The tenant id (The Maison unique identifier).
          schema:
            type: string
          example: car
        - name: cartId
          in: query
          required: true
          description: The cart id
          schema:
            type: string
        - name: validateStock
          in: query
          schema:
            type: boolean
            example: false
            default: false
          description: Add an optional stock validation
      responses:
        '200':
          description: CheckoutCartInfo
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/CheckoutCartInfo'
        '422':
          description: Business error on client side.
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: '#/components/schemas/ProblemDetail'

    CreateReturnItemsRequest:
      type: object

          type: string

          example: 9715328f-49b3-4229-bd39-6c8cf659c50a
        items:
          type: array
          description: A list of items to be returned.
          minItems: 1
          items:
            type: object
            required:
              - lineItemId
              - returnReason
              - returnOnly
            properties:
              lineItemId:
                type: string
                description: The unique identifier of the line item to be returned.
                example: 9715328f-49b3-4229-bd39-6c8cf659c50a
              comment:
                type: string
                description: Optional comment for the return of this specific item, Mandatory for replacement.
                example: Item damaged upon arrival.
              returnReason:
                type: string
                description: The reason for returning the item.
                example: Stolen / Lost order
              operator:
                type: string
                description: Optional identifier for the operator initiating the return.
                example: John Doe
              returnOnly:
                type: boolean
                description: Flag indicating if the return is a return-only (true) or exchange (false).
                example: true
    ProblemDetail:
      type: object
      description: The canonical model for problem details in a JSON [RFC7159] object.
      properties:
        type:
          type: string
          format: URI
          description: |
            A URI reference that identifies the problem type. 
            Usually a link to a documentation or 'about:blank' if not applicable.
          example: about:blank
        title:
          type: string
          description: A short, human-readable summary of the problem type.
          example: Resource not found
        status:
          type: integer
          format: int32
          description: The HTTP status code for this occurrence of the problem.
          example: 400
        detail:
          type: string
          description: A human-readable explanation specific to this occurrence of the problem.
          example: There's no product with id 123 existing in database
        instance:
          type: string
          format: URI
          description: |
            A URI reference that identifies the specific occurrence of the problem. 
            See https://richemont.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/RICCE/pages/176751327/API+Errors for more details.
          example: /service/v1/car/resource
        timestamp:
          type: string
          format: date-time
          description: The date from when the problem occurred, formatted as YYYY-MM-ddThh:mm:ss.fffZ.
          example: '2023-07-24T11:32:28.128Z'
        traceId:
          type: string
          description: The trace ID.
          example: 70b6950950b87a9374f72955c7ffdeb9
    ResetReturnItemsRequest:

This property always denotes the product’s price in the default Merchant’s country, regardless of UseCountryVAT for the end customer’s current country.

content.text:
## OpenAPI Definitions and Controller Architecture

### OpenAPI Specifications Location

The OpenAPI definitions are organized in two locations:

1. **Main OpenAPI Module** (`ce-order-openapi/`):
   - **Main spec**: `ce-order-openapi/openapi/openapi.yaml`
   - **Path definitions**: `ce-order-openapi/openapi/paths/` directory with individual endpoint YAML files
   - **Component schemas**: `ce-order-openapi/openapi/components/schemas/` with domain-specific schemas

2. **Internal OpenAPI for Code Generation** (`ce-order-app/`):
   - **Internal spec**: `ce-order-app/src/main/resources/openapi/openapi-internal.yaml`
   - This is a consolidated version used by the OpenAPI Generator Maven plugin

### Controller Implementation Architecture

The project follows an **OpenAPI-first/Contract-first** approach:

1. **Code Generation Process**:
   - The OpenAPI Generator Maven plugin (v7.9.0) generates Java interfaces from the OpenAPI specifications
   - Generated interfaces are placed in: `ce-order-app/target/generated-sources/openapi/src/main/java/com/richemont/ce_order/api/`
   - Generated models are placed in: `com.richemont.ce_order.model` package

2. **Controller Implementations**:
   
   **OrderController** (`ce-order-app/src/main/java/com/richemont/ce/ce_order/controller/OrderController.java:16`):
   - Implements `OrderApi` interface
   - Endpoint: `POST /{tenant}/order`
   - Currently has minimal implementation (returns 201 CREATED)
   - Security: `SCOPE_order:{tenant}:*:order:create`

   **ReturnController** (`ce-order-app/src/main/java/com/richemont/ce/ce_order/controller/ReturnController.java:17`):
   - Implements `ReturnApi` interface
   - Endpoints:
     - `POST /{tenant}/return` - Create return
     - `POST /{tenant}/return/reset` - Reset return items
   - Full implementation with `ReturnService` integration
   - Security scopes: `order-return:create` and `order-return:update`

   **GlobalEController** (`ce-order-app/src/main/java/com/richemont/ce/ce_order/controller/GlobalEController.java:21`):
   - Does NOT implement a generated interface (direct implementation)
   - Endpoint: `GET /{tenant}/global-e/checkoutCartInfo`
   - Full implementation with `GlobalEService` integration
   - Security: `SCOPE_order:{tenant}:*:cart:read`

### Key Design Patterns

1. **Contract-First Development**: OpenAPI specs define the contract, code is generated from specs
2. **Interface Segregation**: Each controller implements its specific API interface
3. **Security by Default**: All endpoints use OAuth2 with scope-based authorization
4. **Tenant Isolation**: All endpoints are tenant-scoped via path parameter
5. **Standardized Error Handling**: Uses RFC 7807 Problem Details for error responses
in which file is the OrderApi interface defined in open api?

The `OrderApi` interface is defined in the OpenAPI specification at:

**`ce-order-app/src/main/resources/openapi/openapi-internal.yaml`** (lines 26-60)

This defines the `/{tenant}/order` endpoint with:
- Operation ID: `createOrder`
- Method: POST
- Parameters: `tenant` (path) and `swseOrderId` (query)

The interface is then **generated** by the OpenAPI Generator Maven plugin into:

**`ce-order-app/target/generated-sources/openapi/src/main/java/com/richemont/ce_order/api/OrderApi.java`**

The same definition also exists in the main OpenAPI module at:
**`ce-order-openapi/openapi/paths/{tenant}@order.yaml`**

But the code generation uses the `openapi-internal.yaml` file as configured in the pom.xml (line 234):
```xml
<inputSpec>${project.basedir}/src/main/resources/openapi/openapi-internal.yaml</inputSpec>
```

## Plan to Add New Numbers Endpoint

### 1. Update OpenAPI Specification
- Add new endpoint definition to `ce-order-app/src/main/resources/openapi/openapi-internal.yaml`
- Define path `/{tenant}/numbers/get` with GET method
- Add query parameter `type` (required) with enum values
- Define response schema for the generated number

### 2. Generate API Interface
- Run Maven build to generate `NumbersApi` interface from OpenAPI spec
- This will create the interface in `target/generated-sources/openapi/`

### 3. Create Controller Implementation
- Create `NumbersController.java` in the controller package
- Implement the generated `NumbersApi` interface
- Add security annotations matching other controllers
- Wire in any required services for number generation

### 4. Create Service Layer (if needed)
- Create a service class to handle the business logic for number generation
- Implement logic for different number types (orderNumber, ReturnOrderNumber)

This follows the existing contract-first pattern used by OrderController and ReturnController.

The user is asking for a better path than `/numbers/get` for an endpoint that generates numbers based on type (orderNumber, ReturnOrderNumber, etc.). Let me think about what would be more descriptive and follow REST conventions better.

The endpoint is for generating unique identifiers/numbers for different business entities. Some considerations:
1. The purpose is to generate/create new identifiers
2. It's for different types of business entities (orders, returns, etc.)
3. It should follow REST conventions
4. It should be descriptive of the business logic

Better alternatives could be:
- `/identifier/generate` - indicates it's generating identifiers
- `/sequence/next` - indicates it's getting the next in a sequence
- `/reference/generate` - for generating reference numbers
- `/order-number/generate` - but this is too specific if it handles multiple types
- `/identifier` with GET - RESTful way to get a new identifier
- `/reference-number` - business-friendly term
- `/sequence/{type}` - but this changes the structure from query param to path param

Given that this is an order management system and the types are orderNumber and ReturnOrderNumber, these are essentially reference numbers or identifiers for business transactions. The most descriptive would be something that indicates:
1. It's generating something new (not retrieving existing)
2. It's for business reference purposes
3. It can handle different types

I think the best options would be:
- `/reference-number/generate` - clearly indicates generating reference numbers
- `/identifier/generate` - more technical but clear
- `/sequence/next` - indicates getting next in sequence

Since this is for business operations (orders, returns), "reference-number" is probably most descriptive of the business logic.

content.text:
Looking at the business context of generating order numbers and return order numbers, here are better path suggestions:

## More Descriptive Path Options:

1. **`/{tenant}/reference-number/generate`** ✓ Best option
   - Clearly indicates it generates reference numbers for business entities
   - "Reference number" is business-friendly terminology
   - Works well with `?type=orderNumber` or `?type=returnOrderNumber`

2. **`/{tenant}/identifier/generate`**
   - More technical but clear about generating unique identifiers
   - Generic enough for multiple types

3. **`/{tenant}/sequence/next`**
   - Indicates getting the next sequential number
   - Good if these are sequential identifiers

4. **`/{tenant}/order-reference/generate`**
   - More specific to the order domain
   - Still works since "order-reference" can cover both orders and return orders

## Why not `/numbers/get`:
- Too generic - doesn't indicate business purpose
- "get" in the path is redundant with HTTP GET method
- "numbers" doesn't convey that these are business identifiers

## Recommendation:
Use **`/{tenant}/reference-number/generate`** because:
- It's self-documenting about the business purpose
- Follows RESTful conventions (resource/action)
- Aligns with business terminology used in order management systems
- The `generate` action clearly indicates creating new identifiers rather than retrieving existing ones

does reference number describes the fact that what we will implement generates and provides a unique alphanumeric indentifiers for order, return orders, and is open to receiving new types to generate the identifiers?

You make a good point. "Reference number" might be too limiting. Let me reconsider based on your requirements:

## Better Path Options for Unique Alphanumeric Identifier Generation:

1. **`/{tenant}/identifier/generate`** ✓✓ Best option
   - "Identifier" clearly indicates unique alphanumeric values
   - Generic enough to support any business entity type
   - Extensible for future types beyond orders/returns
   - Common terminology in software systems

2. **`/{tenant}/unique-id/generate`** ✓
   - Explicitly states uniqueness
   - Clear about generating IDs
   - Works with `?type=orderNumber`, `?type=returnOrderNumber`, `?type=shipmentId`, etc.

3. **`/{tenant}/business-identifier/generate`**
   - More specific that these are business-domain identifiers
   - Still extensible for new types
   - Perhaps too verbose

4. **`/{tenant}/sequence-id/generate`**
   - Good if these follow a sequential pattern
   - Indicates both sequence and identification purpose

## Why "identifier" is the best choice:
- **Accurate**: Identifiers are exactly what you're generating - unique alphanumeric strings that identify business entities
- **Extensible**: Works for any future type (orders, returns, shipments, invoices, etc.)
- **Industry Standard**: "Identifier" is widely understood in business systems
- **Not restrictive**: Doesn't imply only numeric (like "number") or only reference-style formats

## Final Recommendation:
**`/{tenant}/identifier/generate?type=orderNumber`**

This clearly communicates that the service generates unique identifiers for various business entity types, with the type parameter specifying which pattern/format to use.

get:
  tags:
    - Identifier
  summary: Generate unique identifier
  description: Generates a unique alphanumeric identifier for various business entity types such as orders, return orders, and other configurable types.
  security:
    - oAuth2:
        - order:{tenant}:*:identifier:generate
  operationId: generateIdentifier
  parameters:
    - name: tenant
      in: path
      required: true
      description: The tenant id (The Maison unique identifier).
      schema:
        type: string
      example: car
    - name: type
      in: query
      required: true
      description: The type of identifier to generate. Determines the format and pattern of the generated identifier.
      schema:
        type: string
        enum:
          - orderNumber
          - returnOrderNumber
      example: orderNumber
  responses:
    '200':
      description: Identifier generated successfully.
      content:
        application/json:
          schema:
            $ref: '../components/schemas/identifier/GeneratedIdentifier.yaml'
    '400':
      description: Bad Request - Invalid or unsupported identifier type provided.
      content:
        application/json:
          schema:
            $ref: ../components/schemas/ProblemDetail.yaml
    '500':
      description: Internal Server Error - Unable to generate identifier.
      content:
        application/json:
          schema:
            $ref: ../components/schemas/ProblemDetail.yaml

Based on my analysis of the `ReturnService.java` code, here are the **return number generation rules**:

## Return Number Generation Pattern

The return number follows this format:
```
R + [PREFIX] + [ENV_PREFIX] + [RANDOM_ALPHANUMERIC]
```

### Components:

1. **Fixed Prefix**: Always starts with `"R"` (for Return)

2. **Tenant Prefix**: 
   - Uses `tenantProperties.getOrderNumberPrefix()` if configured
   - Falls back to `tenantProperties.getCode()` if no prefix is set

3. **Environment Prefix** (only for non-production):
   - Added only if `ceProperties.isNonProd()` returns true
   - The prefix comes from `ceProperties.getEnvPrefix()`

4. **Random Alphanumeric String**:
   - **Production**: 7 characters (`SHORT_ID_LENGTH_PRD = 7`)
   - **Non-Production**: 6 characters (`SHORT_ID_LENGTH_NPRD = 6`)
   - Generated using `RandomStringUtils.random()` with letters and numbers
   - Converted to UPPERCASE

### Example Return Numbers:
- Production: `RCAR1A2B3C4` (R + CAR + 7 random chars)
- Non-Production with env prefix "D": `RCARDB1C2D3` (R + CAR + D + 6 random chars)

### Key Implementation Details (lines 493-505):
- The generation uses `RandomStringUtils.random()` with both letters and numbers allowed
- The random part is always converted to uppercase
- The tenant prefix retrieval prioritizes `orderNumberPrefix` over `code`
- Environment detection determines both the env prefix inclusion and random string length

This pattern ensures unique return identifiers while maintaining tenant and environment segregation.

input.title: Identifier Generation Service Implementation Plan
input.content:
# Identifier Generation Service Implementation Plan

## Overview
Implement a unified identifier generation service that supports multiple identifier types (orderNumber, returnOrderNumber) with extensibility for future types.

## Current Generation Patterns

### Order Number Pattern (from ce_payment service)
```
[PREFIX] + [ENV_PREFIX] + [RANDOM_ALPHANUMERIC]
```
- **Prefix**: `tenantProperties.getOrderNumberPrefix()` or fallback to `tenantProperties.getCode()`
- **Environment Prefix**: Added only in non-prod environments
- **Random Part**: 
  - Production: 8 characters
  - Non-Production: 7 characters
  - Alphanumeric, uppercase

### Return Number Pattern (from ReturnService)
```
R + [PREFIX] + [ENV_PREFIX] + [RANDOM_ALPHANUMERIC]
```
- **Fixed Prefix**: "R" (for Return)
- **Prefix**: Same logic as order number
- **Environment Prefix**: Added only in non-prod environments
- **Random Part**:
  - Production: 7 characters
  - Non-Production: 6 characters
  - Alphanumeric, uppercase

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Create IdentifierGeneratorService
Location: `ce-order-app/src/main/java/com/richemont/ce/ce_order/service/IdentifierGeneratorService.java`

#### Core Components:
- Inject `CeProperties` for configuration
- Implement strategy pattern for different identifier types
- Support extensibility for future identifier types

#### Key Methods:
```java
public String generateIdentifier(String tenant, String type)
```

### 2. Identifier Type Strategies

#### OrderNumberStrategy:
- Pattern: `[PREFIX] + [ENV_PREFIX] + [RANDOM]`
- Random length: 8 (prod) / 7 (non-prod)
- No fixed prefix

#### ReturnOrderNumberStrategy:
- Pattern: `R + [PREFIX] + [ENV_PREFIX] + [RANDOM]`
- Random length: 7 (prod) / 6 (non-prod)
- Fixed prefix: "R"

### 3. Configuration Structure

#### Use existing CeProperties:
- `tenantProperties.getOrderNumberPrefix()`
- `tenantProperties.getCode()` (fallback)
- `ceProperties.isNonProd()`
- `ceProperties.getEnvPrefix()`

#### Consider adding configuration for:
- Custom prefixes per identifier type
- Configurable random lengths
- Character sets (alphanumeric vs numeric only)

### 4. Controller Implementation
Location: `ce-order-app/src/main/java/com/richemont/ce/ce_order/controller/IdentifierController.java`

- Implement generated `IdentifierApi` interface
- Handle `GET /{tenant}/identifier/generate?type={type}`
- Return `GeneratedIdentifier` response with:
  - identifier: The generated string
  - type: The type that was generated
  - generatedAt: Timestamp
  - metadata: Optional (tenant, sequence info)

### 5. Validation & Error Handling

#### Validations:
- Valid tenant configuration exists
- Supported identifier type
- Tenant has required permissions

#### Error Cases:
- Invalid/unsupported type → 400 Bad Request
- Tenant not found → 400 Bad Request
- Generation failure → 500 Internal Server Error

### 6. Testing Strategy

#### Unit Tests:
- Test each identifier type strategy
- Test prefix resolution logic
- Test environment detection
- Test random generation lengths

#### Integration Tests:
- Test controller endpoint
- Test different tenant configurations
- Test error scenarios

### 7. Future Extensibility

#### Easy to add new types:
- Create new strategy class
- Add to enum in OpenAPI
- Register in service factory

#### Potential future types:
- shipmentNumber
- invoiceNumber
- refundNumber
- customerNumber

## Implementation Steps

1. Create IdentifierGeneratorService with strategy pattern
2. Implement OrderNumberStrategy and ReturnOrderNumberStrategy
3. Create IdentifierController implementing IdentifierApi
4. Add unit tests for service and strategies
5. Add integration tests for controller
6. Update OpenAPI if needed for additional metadata
7. Document usage and extension points

## Security Considerations
- Ensure generated identifiers are cryptographically random
- Consider rate limiting to prevent abuse
- Audit log generation requests
- Validate tenant access rights

## Performance Considerations
- Random generation is lightweight
- Consider caching tenant configurations
- No database lookups required (stateless generation)

## Migration Notes
- Existing ReturnService.generateReturnNumber() can be refactored to use new service
- Order number generation in ce_payment service remains separate (different service boundary)

input.content:
# Docker Services Ports Reference

## Local Development Services
All services are accessible via `localhost` or `127.0.0.1`

### Database Services

#### MySQL
- **Port**: 3306
- **Container**: mysql-ce
- **Connection**: `mysql://localhost:3306`
- **Usage**: Relational database for structured data

#### MongoDB
- **Ports**: 
  - 27017 (Primary)
  - 27018
  - 27019
- **Container**: mongo_db-ce
- **Connection**: `mongodb://localhost:27017`
- **Usage**: NoSQL database for document storage

### Messaging Services

#### ElasticMQ (AWS SQS Compatible)
- **Ports**:
  - 9324 (REST API)
  - 9325 (Web UI)
- **Container**: elasticMq-ce
- **Connection**: `http://localhost:9324`
- **Web Interface**: `http://localhost:9325`
- **Usage**: Local SQS queue emulation for async messaging

#### Solace PubSub
- **Admin Console**: 8180 (maps to internal 8080)
- **Messaging Ports**:
  - 1443 (HTTPS)
  - 1883 (MQTT)
  - 1943 (SEMP/HTTP)
  - 2222 (SSH)
  - 5671 (AMQP over TLS)
  - 5672 (AMQP)
  - 8000 (REST/HTTP)
  - 8008 (Web transport)
  - 8443 (REST/HTTPS)
  - 8883 (MQTT over TLS)
  - 9443 (SEMP over TLS)
  - 55003 (SMF compressed)
  - 55443 (SMF over TLS)
  - 55554 (Health check, maps to internal 55555)
- **Container**: pubSubStandardSingleNode-ce
- **Admin URL**: `http://localhost:8180`
- **Usage**: Enterprise messaging broker for event streaming

### Testing Services

#### MockServer
- **Port**: 1080
- **Container**: mockServer-ce
- **Connection**: `http://localhost:1080`
- **Usage**: HTTP/HTTPS mock server for API testing
- **Note**: Running on linux/amd64 platform (may show warning on ARM64)

## Docker Compose Management

### Start Services
```bash
cd /Users/caio.niehues/IdeaProjects/docker
docker compose -f docker-compose-lrp.yaml up -d
```

### Stop Services
```bash
docker compose -f docker-compose-lrp.yaml down
```

### View Service Status
```bash
docker compose -f docker-compose-lrp.yaml ps
```

### View Logs
```bash
# All services
docker compose -f docker-compose-lrp.yaml logs

# Specific service
docker compose -f docker-compose-lrp.yaml logs [service-name]
```

## Configuration Files

### ElasticMQ Configuration
- **File**: `/Users/caio.niehues/IdeaProjects/docker/mqcustom.conf`
- **Mount**: `/opt/elasticmq.conf` (inside container)

## Common Connection Strings

### Spring Boot Application Properties
```yaml
# MySQL
spring.datasource.url: jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ce_order

# MongoDB
spring.data.mongodb.uri: mongodb://localhost:27017/ce_order

# ElasticMQ (SQS)
aws.sqs.endpoint: http://localhost:9324

# Solace
solace.java.host: tcp://localhost:55554
```

## Troubleshooting

### Port Already in Use
If a port is already in use, either:
1. Stop the conflicting service
2. Modify the port mapping in `docker-compose-lrp.yaml`

### Container Won't Start
Check logs: `docker compose -f docker-compose-lrp.yaml logs [container-name]`

### ElasticMQ Mount Error
Ensure `mqcustom.conf` is a file, not a directory

## Notes
- All services are configured for local development
- Data persistence volumes are managed by Docker
- The `version` attribute warning in docker-compose can be ignored

Based on my examination of the codebase:

## **No, this project does NOT use MongoDB for persistence**

The ce-order service is actually **stateless** and uses **Commercetools** as its persistence layer. Here's what I found:

### Architecture:
1. **No Database Dependencies**: 
   - No MongoDB, MySQL, or JPA dependencies in pom.xml
   - No @Entity, @Repository, or @Document annotations
   - No datasource configurations

2. **Uses Commercetools as Backend**:
   - Dependencies: `commercetools.version>17.27.0` and `ce-commercetools-spring-boot-starter`
   - Multiple Commercetools client classes (CTOrderClient, CTCartClient, etc.)
   - All data operations go through Commercetools APIs

3. **Event-Driven via Solace**:
   - Uses Solace for messaging (`spring-cloud-starter-stream-solace`)
   - Processes events from Commercetools
   - No local state storage

### What the Service Does:
- **Reads** from Commercetools (orders, carts, customers, payments)
- **Writes** to Commercetools (order updates, state transitions)
- **Processes** events via Solace messaging
- **Exposes** REST APIs for order operations

The MongoDB in the Docker Compose file is likely:
- Used by other services in the ecosystem
- A legacy requirement
- Or for future features

For the **identifier generation** feature you're implementing, it will also be **stateless** - generating identifiers algorithmically without database lookups, similar to how the return numbers are currently generated.

## MongoDB-Based Identifier Generation Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Add MongoDB Infrastructure
1. **Add Spring Data MongoDB dependency** to pom.xml
2. **Configure MongoDB connection** in application.yml and application-local.yml
3. **Create entity model** `GeneratedIdentifierEntity` with compound unique index on (identifier, type)
4. **Create repository interface** extending MongoRepository

### Phase 2: Implement Persistence Logic
1. **Create IdentifierGeneratorService** with:
   - Retry logic (5 attempts) for handling collisions
   - Generate identifier → Attempt save → Retry on duplicate
   - Status management (RESERVED, ACTIVE, CANCELLED)
2. **Create IdentifierController** implementing the generated API
3. **Add proper error handling** for connection failures and max retries

### Phase 3: Database Setup
1. **Create MongoDB indexes**:
   - Compound unique index on (identifier, type)
   - Single indexes on type, tenant, status
   - Optional TTL index for automatic cleanup
2. **Initialize collection** with proper constraints

### Key Features:
- **Guaranteed uniqueness** via MongoDB unique constraint
- **Retry mechanism** for collision handling (max 5 attempts)
- **Audit trail** with createdAt, createdBy, usedBy fields
- **Status tracking** for identifier lifecycle
- **Tenant isolation** in multi-tenant environment

### Document Schema:
```javascript
{
  identifier: "CARD123ABC",
  type: "orderNumber",
  tenant: "car",
  status: "active",
  createdAt: ISODate(),
  metadata: { /* flexible */ }
}
```

This approach ensures uniqueness while maintaining the existing identifier generation patterns (order numbers and return numbers) with proper persistence.

## Create Global CLAUDE.md Coding Guidelines

### Plan Overview
Create a comprehensive CLAUDE.md file at the project root that establishes coding standards for the ce-order project, with **SIMPLICITY as the #1 priority**.

### File Location
`/Users/caio.niehues/IdeaProjects/ce-order/CLAUDE.md` (replacing the existing one)

### Structure of the New CLAUDE.md

1. **🎯 SIMPLICITY FIRST PRINCIPLE**
   - The 3-Question Rule before writing ANY code
   - Complexity scoring system (keep < 5)
   - KISS principle as the foundation

2. **📊 DECISION MATRIX**
   - Simple solutions vs over-engineered patterns
   - When to use direct implementation vs abstraction
   - Real examples from the codebase

3. **✅ CLEAN CODE STANDARDS**
   - Meaningful names (no abbreviations)
   - Small functions (< 20 lines)
   - Single responsibility
   - All parameters must be final
   - No wildcard imports

4. **🚫 ANTI-PATTERNS TO AVOID**
   - Factory madness
   - Premature abstraction
   - Deep inheritance
   - Over-configured solutions

5. **🏗️ EFFECTIVE JAVA PATTERNS**
   - Static factory methods over constructors
   - Builder pattern for complex objects
   - Favor composition over inheritance
   - Use enums instead of constants
   - Modern Java 21 features (records, pattern matching)

6. **🔴 TDD PRACTICES**
   - Red → Green → Refactor cycle
   - Test naming: methodName_condition_expectedResult
   - Let exceptions bubble up
   - Only catch when you can recover

7. **📏 PROJECT-SPECIFIC RULES**
   - Spring Boot conventions
   - Commercetools integration patterns
   - Identifier generation patterns
   - MongoDB persistence guidelines

8. **✔️ THE SIMPLICITY CHECKLIST**
   - Can I explain this in one sentence?
   - Would a junior understand this?
   - Am I solving a real problem?
   - Is my complexity score < 5?

### Key Emphasis
The document will prominently feature **SIMPLICITY** as the overriding principle, with examples showing simple solutions vs over-engineered ones, making it clear that we should always choose the simpler approach unless there's a compelling reason not to.

This will become the definitive guide for all code written in this project, ensuring consistency and maintainability.

## Create Global CLAUDE.md Coding Guidelines

### Plan Overview
Create a comprehensive global CLAUDE.md file that establishes coding standards for ALL projects, with **SIMPLICITY as the #1 priority**.

### File Location
`/Users/caio.niehues/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (global configuration)

### Structure of the Global CLAUDE.md

1. **🎯 SIMPLICITY FIRST PRINCIPLE**
   - The 3-Question Rule before writing ANY code:
     1. Can I use what already exists? → DO THAT
     2. Can I solve this with a simple method? → DO THAT  
     3. Do I really need this abstraction NOW? → PROBABLY NOT
   - Complexity scoring system (keep < 5)
   - KISS principle as the foundation

2. **📊 SIMPLICITY DECISION MATRIX**
   - Direct implementation vs patterns
   - When NOT to abstract
   - Real-world examples of simple > complex

3. **✅ JAVA CLEAN CODE STANDARDS**
   - Meaningful, searchable names
   - Functions < 20 lines
   - Single responsibility
   - All parameters must be final
   - NO wildcard imports
   - NO null returns (use Optional)
   - NO defensive try-catch

4. **🚫 ANTI-PATTERNS TO AVOID**
   - Factory madness
   - Premature abstraction  
   - Abstraction addiction
   - Deep inheritance (> 1 level)
   - Configuration complexity

5. **🏗️ EFFECTIVE JAVA PATTERNS**
   - Static factory methods
   - Builder pattern (when needed)
   - Composition over inheritance
   - Enums over constants
   - Modern Java 21: records, pattern matching, text blocks

6. **🔴 TDD PRACTICES**
   - Red → Green → Refactor
   - Test naming convention
   - Let exceptions bubble up
   - Only catch when you can recover

7. **✔️ THE SIMPLICITY CHECKLIST**
   Before committing ANY code:
   - [ ] Can I explain this in one sentence?
   - [ ] Would a junior understand this?
   - [ ] Am I solving a real problem?
   - [ ] Have I avoided unnecessary abstractions?
   - [ ] Is my complexity score < 5?

8. **💡 REMEMBER**
   - Clean code is simple, readable, and testable
   - Start simple, refactor when needed
   - Use built-in features first
   - Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication

### Key Emphasis
This global CLAUDE.md will apply to ALL projects, establishing **SIMPLICITY** as the overriding principle across all codebases. Examples will clearly show simple solutions are preferred over over-engineered ones.

The document will be a synthesis of:
- `/Users/caio.niehues/.claude/docs/java-standards.md`
- `/Users/caio.niehues/.claude/docs/simplicity-principles.md`

With SIMPLICITY taking precedence over all other principles.
